notes.heading: Thomson's Model of Atom
notes.text: After the discovery of the electron, J.J. Thomson proposed a model to explain how positive and negative charges are arranged inside an atom. According to Thomson, an atom is a positively charged sphere in which electrons are embedded. This model is also called the plum pudding model or watermelon model.
points: The positive charge is spread uniformly throughout the atom.
Electrons are embedded inside the positively charged sphere.
Total positive charge is equal to total negative charge, so the atom is electrically neutral.
Neutrality of atom: +q + (-q) = 0.
In the watermelon comparison, the red part represents positive charge and the seeds represent electrons.
Success: Thomson's model explained why atoms are electrically neutral.
Failure: It could not explain Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment.
According to Thomson's model, heavy alpha particles should pass almost straight through the atom because positive charge is spread throughout the atom.
Rutherford's experiment showed that some alpha particles were strongly deflected and a few even bounced back, proving that positive charge is concentrated in a very small region called the nucleus.
Key idea: Atom = positive sphere + embedded electrons.

notes.heading: Rutherford's Alpha-Particle Scattering Experiment
notes.text: Rutherford performed the alpha-particle scattering experiment to study the internal structure of the atom. He bombarded a very thin gold foil with alpha particles and observed their deflections using a fluorescent screen.
points: Alpha particles are helium nuclei: ⁴₂He²⁺.
Alpha particles have charge +2e, are heavy compared to electrons, move with high speed, and are emitted by radioactive substances.
Experimental setup: source of alpha particles, very thin gold foil, and circular fluorescent screen around the foil.
When alpha particles struck the fluorescent screen, small flashes of light were seen.
Observation 1: Most alpha particles passed straight through the gold foil without any deflection.
Conclusion from observation 1: Most of the atom is empty space.
Observation 2: Some alpha particles were deflected through small angles.
Conclusion from observation 2: A positive charge exists inside the atom and repels alpha particles.
Observation 3: Very few alpha particles were deflected through very large angles, and some even came back.
Conclusion from observation 3: Positive charge and most of the mass of the atom are concentrated in a very small central region called the nucleus.
Rutherford used gold foil because gold is highly malleable and can be made into an extremely thin sheet.
If atomic size is about 10⁻¹⁰ m, nuclear size is about 10⁻¹⁵ m.
Key conclusion: Atom has a small, dense, positively charged nucleus, and most of the atom is empty space.

notes.heading: Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom
notes.text: Based on the alpha-particle scattering experiment, Rutherford proposed the nuclear model of the atom. According to this model, the atom contains a small, dense, positively charged nucleus at the centre, and electrons revolve around it.
points: The nucleus contains almost all the mass of the atom and the positive charge of the atom.
Electrons revolve around the nucleus in circular paths.
This model is also called the planetary model of the atom because electrons revolve around the nucleus like planets revolve around the Sun.
The size of the nucleus is very small compared to the size of the atom.
Atomic size ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ m and nuclear size ≈ 10⁻¹⁵ m.
Electrons are negatively charged and the nucleus is positively charged, so electrostatic force of attraction acts between them.
This electrostatic force provides the centripetal force required for circular motion of electrons.
Electrostatic force = Centripetal force.
Success: Rutherford's model explained why most alpha particles passed straight, why some were deflected, and why a few bounced back.
Failure: According to classical electromagnetic theory, a charged particle moving in a circle is accelerated and should continuously emit radiation.
If electrons continuously lose energy, their orbits should become smaller and they should finally fall into the nucleus.
This would make the atom collapse, but atoms are stable in reality.
Rutherford's model could not explain the stability of atoms.
Rutherford's model could not explain the line spectrum of hydrogen atom because it predicted continuous radiation.
Key idea: Atom = small positive nucleus + revolving electrons.

notes.heading: Atomic Spectra
notes.text: A spectrum is obtained when light is separated into its different wavelengths or colours. Atomic spectra help us understand the energy levels of atoms.
points: When white light is passed through a prism, it splits into different colours. This band of colours is called a spectrum.
A continuous spectrum contains all wavelengths without any gap.
Examples of continuous spectrum: sunlight and light from a hot filament bulb.
A line spectrum contains only some specific wavelengths and appears as separate bright or dark lines.
A line spectrum shows that atoms emit or absorb only fixed amounts of energy.
Emission spectrum: The spectrum obtained when an atom gives out light.
Example: Excited hydrogen gas emits light that contains only certain wavelengths, giving a line emission spectrum.
Absorption spectrum: The spectrum obtained when white light passes through a cool gas and the gas absorbs certain wavelengths.
An absorption spectrum has dark lines on a bright background.
Hydrogen absorbs only those wavelengths which it can emit.
Each element has a unique spectrum, so the spectrum acts like a fingerprint of the element.
Atomic spectra help identify elements such as hydrogen, helium, sodium and mercury.
Rutherford's model could not explain why hydrogen produces only specific spectral lines instead of a continuous spectrum.
Key idea: Fixed energy changes in atoms produce line spectra.

notes.heading: Hydrogen Spectrum and Spectral Series
notes.text: Hydrogen is the simplest atom, containing one proton in the nucleus and one electron around it. When hydrogen gas is excited, it emits light and gives a line spectrum.
points: Hydrogen does not produce a continuous spectrum; it produces separate bright lines of definite wavelengths.
This shows that the electron in hydrogen can have only certain fixed energy values.
The spectral lines of hydrogen are grouped into series.
Each series is formed when the electron jumps from higher energy levels to one fixed lower energy level.
Lyman series: Electron jumps from n = 2, 3, 4, ... to n = 1. This series lies in the ultraviolet region.
Balmer series: Electron jumps from n = 3, 4, 5, ... to n = 2. This series lies in the visible region.
Paschen series: Electron jumps from n = 4, 5, 6, ... to n = 3. This series lies in the infrared region.
Brackett series: Electron jumps from higher levels to n = 4. This series lies in the infrared region.
Pfund series: Electron jumps from higher levels to n = 5. This series lies in the infrared region.
Rydberg formula: 1/λ = R(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²).
In the Rydberg formula, λ is wavelength, R is the Rydberg constant, n_i is the initial higher energy level, and n_f is the final lower energy level.
Rydberg constant: R = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.
Condition: n_i > n_f.
For Balmer series, n_f = 2, so 1/λ = R(1/2² - 1/n_i²), where n_i = 3, 4, 5, ...
Key idea: n_f = 1 gives Lyman series, n_f = 2 gives Balmer series, and n_f = 3 gives Paschen series.

notes.heading: Bohr's Model of Hydrogen Atom
notes.text: Niels Bohr proposed a model of the hydrogen atom to explain atomic stability and the line spectrum of hydrogen. Bohr introduced the idea of stationary orbits and quantised energy levels.
points: Bohr's first postulate: Electrons revolve around the nucleus only in certain fixed circular orbits called stationary orbits or permitted orbits.
An electron does not lose energy while moving in a stationary orbit, so the atom remains stable.
Each permitted orbit has a fixed energy.
The orbit closer to the nucleus has lower energy and the orbit farther from the nucleus has higher energy.
Energy order: E₁ < E₂ < E₃ < E₄.
Bohr's second postulate: Angular momentum of electron is quantised.
Quantisation condition: mvr = nh/2π.
Here m is mass of electron, v is velocity of electron, r is radius of orbit, h is Planck's constant, and n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ...
n is called the principal quantum number.
Bohr's third postulate: Energy is emitted or absorbed only when an electron jumps from one orbit to another.
For transition from higher orbit to lower orbit, energy is emitted: E_i - E_f = hν.
For transition from lower orbit to higher orbit, energy is absorbed: E_f - E_i = hν.
Because only fixed energy levels are allowed, only fixed wavelengths are emitted. This explains the hydrogen line spectrum.
Bohr's model explained the stability of hydrogen atom, hydrogen line spectrum, Rydberg formula, and energy levels of hydrogen atom.
Limitation: Bohr's model works well only for hydrogen and hydrogen-like atoms having one electron, such as H, He⁺, Li²⁺ and Be³⁺.
Bohr's model does not properly explain spectra of atoms having many electrons.

notes.heading: Radius of Bohr Orbit
notes.text: In Bohr's model, the electron revolves around the nucleus in fixed circular orbits. Each orbit has a definite radius called the Bohr orbit radius.
points: In the hydrogen atom, electrostatic attraction between the proton and the electron provides the centripetal force for circular motion.
Force equation: (1/4πε₀)(e²/r²) = mv²/r.
Bohr's quantisation condition: mvr = nh/2π, where n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ...
Radius of the nth orbit: r_n = n²h²ε₀/(πme²).
For hydrogen atom: r_n = n²a₀.
a₀ is called the Bohr radius.
Bohr radius is the radius of the first orbit of hydrogen atom.
First orbit: r₁ = a₀ = 0.529 Å.
Second orbit: r₂ = 2²a₀ = 4a₀.
Third orbit: r₃ = 3²a₀ = 9a₀.
Ratio of radii: r₁ : r₂ : r₃ = 1 : 4 : 9.
For hydrogen-like atoms: r_n = n²a₀/Z, where Z is the atomic number.
For He⁺, Z = 2, so r_n = n²a₀/2.
Key idea: For hydrogen, r_n = n²a₀, so radius increases as n increases.

notes.heading: Velocity of Electron in Bohr Orbit
notes.text: In Bohr's model, an electron has a definite velocity in each permitted orbit. The velocity depends on the orbit number and the atomic number for hydrogen-like atoms.
points: Electrostatic attraction between proton and electron provides centripetal force.
Force equation: (1/4πε₀)(e²/r²) = mv²/r.
From this, v² = (1/4πε₀)(e²/mr).
Bohr's quantisation condition: mvr = nh/2π.
Using the radius formula, velocity of electron in the nth orbit is v_n = e²/(2ε₀nh).
For hydrogen atom: v_n = v₁/n.
Velocity in the first orbit: v₁ = 2.18 × 10⁶ m/s.
Therefore v_n = (2.18 × 10⁶)/n m/s for hydrogen.
For first orbit, v₁ = 2.18 × 10⁶ m/s.
For second orbit, v₂ = v₁/2.
For third orbit, v₃ = v₁/3.
Ratio of velocities: v₁ : v₂ : v₃ = 1 : 1/2 : 1/3.
As the electron moves to higher orbits, radius increases and velocity decreases.
The electron is fastest in the first orbit.
For hydrogen-like atoms: v_n = (Z/n)v₁, where Z is atomic number.
For He⁺, Z = 2, so v_n = 2v₁/n.
Key idea: For hydrogen atom, v_n = v₁/n.

notes.heading: Energy of Electron in Bohr Orbit
notes.text: In Bohr's model, the electron has a definite energy in each orbit. The total energy is the sum of kinetic energy and potential energy.
points: Total energy: E = K.E. + P.E.
Using electrostatic force as centripetal force: (1/4πε₀)(e²/r²) = mv²/r.
Multiplying by r/2 gives kinetic energy: K.E. = (1/8πε₀)(e²/r).
Potential energy of electron in the electric field of nucleus: P.E. = -(1/4πε₀)(e²/r).
The negative sign shows that the electron is bound to the nucleus.
Total energy: E = (1/8πε₀)(e²/r) - (1/4πε₀)(e²/r).
Therefore E = -(1/8πε₀)(e²/r).
The total energy of the electron is negative because it is bound to the nucleus.
Energy of nth orbit for hydrogen atom: E_n = -13.6/n² eV.
For first orbit: E₁ = -13.6 eV.
For second orbit: E₂ = -13.6/2² = -3.4 eV.
For third orbit: E₃ = -13.6/3² = -1.51 eV.
When n = ∞, E∞ = 0, meaning the electron is free from the atom.
For hydrogen-like atoms: E_n = -13.6Z²/n² eV.
For He⁺, Z = 2, so E_n = -54.4/n² eV.
Key idea: For hydrogen atom, E_n = -13.6/n² eV. As n increases, energy becomes less negative and approaches zero.

notes.heading: Energy Level Diagram and Transitions
notes.text: The fixed energies of an electron in a hydrogen atom are represented by an energy level diagram. Transitions between energy levels produce absorption or emission of radiation.
points: Energy of nth orbit in hydrogen: E_n = -13.6/n² eV.
For n = 1, E₁ = -13.6 eV. This is the ground state.
For n = 2, E₂ = -3.4 eV.
For n = 3, E₃ = -1.51 eV.
For n = 4, E₄ = -0.85 eV.
For n = ∞, E∞ = 0. At this state, the electron is completely free from the atom.
Ground state: The lowest energy state of an atom. For hydrogen, it is n = 1 with energy -13.6 eV.
Excited state: A higher energy state reached when an electron absorbs energy and jumps to a higher orbit.
The state n = 2 is the first excited state of hydrogen.
The state n = 3 is the second excited state of hydrogen.
Transition means the jump of an electron from one energy level to another.
Absorption transition: Electron absorbs energy and jumps from lower level to higher level.
Energy absorbed = E_higher - E_lower.
Emission transition: Electron jumps from higher level to lower level and emits energy.
Energy emitted = E_higher - E_lower.
For emission from E_i to E_f: ΔE = E_i - E_f = hν.
Frequency of emitted radiation: ν = (E_i - E_f)/h.
Example: For transition n = 2 to n = 1, E₂ = -3.4 eV and E₁ = -13.6 eV.
Energy emitted = E₂ - E₁ = -3.4 - (-13.6) = 10.2 eV.
Ionisation energy is the energy required to remove the electron completely from the atom.
For hydrogen, ionisation from n = 1 to n = ∞ requires 0 - (-13.6) = 13.6 eV.
Key idea: When an electron jumps down, energy is emitted. When it jumps up, energy is absorbed.

notes.heading: Excitation Energy and Ionisation Energy
notes.text: An electron can move from one energy level to another by absorbing or emitting energy. Two important terms related to this are excitation energy and ionisation energy.
points: Excitation: When an electron absorbs energy and jumps from a lower orbit to a higher orbit, the atom is said to be excited.
Example of excitation in hydrogen: n = 1 → n = 2.
Excitation energy is the minimum energy required to take an electron from the ground state to any higher energy state.
For hydrogen, E_n = -13.6/n² eV.
Ground state energy: E₁ = -13.6 eV.
First excited state energy: E₂ = -3.4 eV.
First excitation energy = E₂ - E₁ = -3.4 - (-13.6) = 10.2 eV.
Second excited state is n = 3 with energy E₃ = -1.51 eV.
Second excitation energy = E₃ - E₁ = -1.51 - (-13.6) = 12.09 eV.
Ionisation means removing the electron completely from the atom.
For hydrogen, ionisation means taking the electron from n = 1 to n = ∞.
At n = ∞, E∞ = 0 and the electron is free.
Ionisation energy is the minimum energy required to remove an electron completely from the atom in its ground state.
Ionisation energy of hydrogen = E∞ - E₁ = 0 - (-13.6) = 13.6 eV.
Excitation means the electron goes to a higher orbit but remains in the atom.
Ionisation means the electron leaves the atom completely.
For hydrogen, excitation energy is less than 13.6 eV, while ionisation energy is 13.6 eV.

notes.heading: De Broglie Explanation of Bohr's Quantisation Condition
notes.text: Bohr gave the angular momentum quantisation condition, but de Broglie later explained why only certain orbits are allowed by using the wave nature of electrons.
points: Bohr's quantisation condition: mvr = nh/2π.
According to de Broglie, every moving particle has wave nature.
An electron moving around the nucleus also behaves like a wave.
De Broglie wavelength of a moving electron: λ = h/mv.
Here λ is de Broglie wavelength, h is Planck's constant, m is mass of electron, and v is velocity of electron.
In a stable orbit, the electron wave must form a standing wave around the nucleus.
For a standing wave to exist, the circumference of the orbit must contain a whole number of wavelengths.
Allowed orbit condition: 2πr = nλ, where n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ...
Substituting λ = h/mv in 2πr = nλ gives 2πr = nh/mv.
Multiplying by mv gives 2πmvr = nh.
Therefore mvr = nh/2π, which is Bohr's quantisation condition.
Only those orbits are allowed where the electron wave fits completely around the orbit.
If the electron wave does not fit properly, destructive interference occurs and the orbit is not stable.
Allowed examples: 2πr = 1λ, 2λ, 3λ, ...
Not allowed examples: 2πr = 1.5λ, 2.3λ, ...
Key idea: Stable electron orbits are those in which the electron wave forms a standing wave.

notes.heading: Limitations of Bohr's Model
notes.text: Bohr's model was successful for the hydrogen atom and hydrogen-like atoms, but it could not explain many features of complex atoms and modern quantum behaviour.
points: Bohr's model works properly only for atoms or ions having one electron, such as H, He⁺ and Li²⁺.
It fails for atoms having more than one electron, such as He, Li, Be, O and Na.
In multi-electron atoms, electron-electron repulsion also occurs, which Bohr's model does not handle properly.
Bohr's model cannot explain the fine structure of spectral lines.
Fine structure means splitting of a spectral line into many closely spaced lines when observed with high-resolution instruments.
Bohr's model cannot explain the Zeeman effect.
Zeeman effect is the splitting of spectral lines when an atom is placed in a magnetic field.
Bohr's model cannot explain the Stark effect.
Stark effect is the splitting of spectral lines when an atom is placed in an electric field.
Bohr's model does not include the wave nature of electrons properly.
Bohr assumed electrons move in fixed circular orbits, but modern quantum mechanics describes electrons using orbitals and probability.
Bohr's fixed orbit idea is against Heisenberg's uncertainty principle.
Heisenberg's uncertainty principle says that position and momentum cannot both be known exactly at the same time.
Bohr's model cannot explain the relative intensities of spectral lines, meaning why some lines are bright and some are faint.

notes.heading: Important Formulas from Atoms Chapter
notes.text: The Atoms chapter contains important formulas related to hydrogen spectrum, Bohr radius, velocity, energy, transitions, ionisation and de Broglie wavelength.
points: Rydberg formula for hydrogen spectrum: 1/λ = R(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²), where n_i > n_f.
Rydberg constant: R = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹.
Bohr radius formula for hydrogen: r_n = n²a₀.
Bohr radius: a₀ = 0.529 × 10⁻¹⁰ m = 0.529 Å.
Radius for hydrogen-like atoms: r_n = n²a₀/Z.
Velocity of electron in hydrogen atom: v_n = v₁/n.
Velocity in first orbit: v₁ = 2.18 × 10⁶ m/s.
Velocity for hydrogen-like atoms: v_n = (Z/n)v₁.
Energy of electron in hydrogen atom: E_n = -13.6/n² eV.
Energy of electron in hydrogen-like atoms: E_n = -13.6Z²/n² eV.
Energy difference during transition: ΔE = E_i - E_f.
Photon energy relation: ΔE = hν = hc/λ.
Frequency of radiation: ν = ΔE/h.
Wavelength of radiation: λ = hc/ΔE.
If ΔE is in eV, convert using 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J when using SI units.
Ionisation energy of hydrogen from ground state: n = 1 → n = ∞, so ionisation energy = 13.6 eV.
Excitation energy from ground state to nth state: ΔE = E_n - E₁.
First excitation energy of hydrogen: n = 1 → n = 2, ΔE = -3.4 - (-13.6) = 10.2 eV.
De Broglie wavelength of electron: λ = h/mv.
Allowed orbit condition: 2πr = nλ.
Bohr quantisation condition from de Broglie wave: mvr = nh/2π.

notes.heading: Important Numerical Concepts from Atoms Chapter
notes.text: Numericals from the Atoms chapter are mainly based on energy levels, transition energy, wavelength, spectral series, ionisation energy, excitation energy and radius of Bohr orbits.
points: Energy of nth orbit of hydrogen: E_n = -13.6/n² eV.
Energy emitted during transition from higher level to lower level: ΔE = E_i - E_f.
Example: For transition n = 3 to n = 2, E₃ = -13.6/3² = -1.51 eV and E₂ = -13.6/2² = -3.4 eV.
Energy emitted for n = 3 to n = 2: ΔE = -1.51 - (-3.4) = 1.89 eV.
Photon energy formula: E = hc/λ.
Useful formula in electron volt and nanometre: E(eV) = 1240/λ(nm).
Wavelength formula: λ(nm) = 1240/E(eV).
Example: If photon energy is 10.2 eV, λ = 1240/10.2 = 121.6 nm. This belongs to the Lyman series.
To identify the spectral series, check the final orbit n_f.
n_f = 1 gives Lyman series in ultraviolet region.
n_f = 2 gives Balmer series in visible region.
n_f = 3 gives Paschen series in infrared region.
n_f = 4 gives Brackett series in infrared region.
n_f = 5 gives Pfund series in infrared region.
Example: Transition n = 5 to n = 2 belongs to the Balmer series because n_f = 2.
Ionisation energy for hydrogen from ground state: E∞ - E₁ = 0 - (-13.6) = 13.6 eV.
First excitation energy for hydrogen: n = 1 to n = 2 gives ΔE = E₂ - E₁ = -3.4 - (-13.6) = 10.2 eV.
Radius of nth orbit of hydrogen: r_n = n²a₀, where a₀ = 0.529 Å.
Example: Radius of third orbit r₃ = 3²a₀ = 9 × 0.529 = 4.761 Å.
Quick method for transitions: find E_i = -13.6/n_i², find E_f = -13.6/n_f², calculate ΔE = E_i - E_f, then use λ = 1240/ΔE for wavelength in nm.

notes.heading: Practice Numericals from Atoms Chapter
notes.text: The following solved examples show the main numerical methods used in the Atoms chapter.
points: Numerical 1: Energy of electron in second orbit of hydrogen. E₂ = -13.6/2² = -13.6/4 = -3.4 eV.
Numerical 2: Energy emitted when electron jumps from n = 3 to n = 2. E₃ = -1.51 eV, E₂ = -3.4 eV, so ΔE = E₃ - E₂ = -1.51 - (-3.4) = 1.89 eV.
Numerical 3: Wavelength of photon of energy 10.2 eV. λ(nm) = 1240/E(eV) = 1240/10.2 = 121.6 nm. This lies in the ultraviolet region.
Numerical 4: Radius of second orbit of hydrogen. r₂ = 2²a₀ = 4a₀ = 4 × 0.529 = 2.116 Å.
Numerical 5: First excitation energy of hydrogen. n = 1 to n = 2, E₁ = -13.6 eV, E₂ = -3.4 eV, so ΔE = E₂ - E₁ = -3.4 - (-13.6) = 10.2 eV.
Numerical 6: Ionisation energy of hydrogen. n = 1 to n = ∞, E₁ = -13.6 eV and E∞ = 0, so ΔE = 0 - (-13.6) = 13.6 eV.
Numerical 7: Transition n = 4 to n = 2 belongs to the Balmer series because final orbit n_f = 2. Balmer series lies in the visible region.
Numerical 8: Energy of electron in He⁺ ion for n = 1. For hydrogen-like atoms, E_n = -13.6Z²/n² eV. For He⁺, Z = 2 and n = 1, so E₁ = -13.6 × 2² = -54.4 eV.
Important formulas for practice: E_n = -13.6/n² eV, ΔE = E_i - E_f, λ(nm) = 1240/E(eV), r_n = n²a₀, and E_n = -13.6Z²/n² eV for hydrogen-like atoms.
